All of our happiness comes from God’s mercy; therefore, the first thing we pray for is that God will have mercy on us, on us who are sinners, and forgive our sins. Forgiveness is conveyed by God's blessing, and secured in that. If we walk with God through faith, we may hope that his face will shine upon us. The psalmist moves on to a prayer for the conversion of the Gentiles, which shows that the Old Testament saints desired that their advantages might also be enjoyed by others. And many Scripture prophecies and promises are wrapped up in prayers: the answer to the church’s prayer is as sure as the fulfillment of God's promises. The joy wished to the nations is holy joy. Let them be glad that, through his providence, the Lord will control the affairs of kingdoms; that even the kingdoms of this world will become the kingdom of the Lord and of his Christ. Then a joyful prospect of all good is declared when God does this. The success of the gospel brings outward mercies with it; righteousness exalts a nation. The blessing of the Lord sweetens all our earthly comforts to us, and makes them comforts indeed. All the world will be brought to worship Him. When the gospel begins to spread, it will go forward more and more until it reaches the ends of the earth. It is good to join ourselves with those who are blessed by the Lord. If nothing had been spoken in Scripture regarding the conversion of the heathen, we might think it vain to attempt so hopeless a work. But when we see with what confidence it is declared in the Scriptures, we may engage in missionary labors, assured that God will fulfill his own word. And should we be reluctant to make known to the heathen the knowledge with which we are favored and the salvation we profess to glory in? They cannot learn unless they are taught. Then let us go forward in the strength of the Lord, and look to him to accompany the word with the Holy Spirit; then Satan’s kingdom will be destroyed, and the kingdom of our Redeemer established.
